speaker: interviewer
text: Again, what it was like when you arrived in London and when this lady picked you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember much. I don't-- know it's amazing how little-- I'm always amazed myself how little I remember. I just remember some incidents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They looked-- I think she looked after us as well as she possibly could. There was no-- I think what I missed mainly-- I-- I do, I've been told this afterwards by somebody, that I-- I missed-- I must have missed my parents terribly because I mean, a child gets cuddled and kissed. That, of course, didn't happen. They didn't know how to-- well, I probably would have rejected it anyway. But I do remember an incident that I was told much later in my life. I met a man who is a-- a-- a distant cousin of a cousin of mine and he told me that-- that I told him how much I missed this. But I don't honestly remember it. The house that I lived in was a very nice house. It was in a very nice area. I was sent to an English church school, it was called Holy Trinity Church School in East Finchley. My sister was far brighter than I was, went to a very good school, and it's a very well-known school in the Hampstead Gardens suburb called Henrietta Barnett School. But I went to this little school where I was very upset because I was put in with the babies because I couldn't speak English. And until I learned some English and gradually attained my own class, my age group. And I remember I used to get a penny a day-- not-- not the penny-- not today's penny, the old penny, which I was allowed-- if I was a good girl, which wasn't so often I didn't get it. But this penny I was allowed to spend how I liked. I could buy a banana with this, I could buy my milk at school with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And apart-- apart from that, I only remember odd incidents. Because when the blitz started-- I remember the day war broke out, funnily enough, which was a Sunday-- I think. And I happened to be at the rabbi's house, I don't know why, maybe to-- for Hebrew lessons, possibly? And I came out of the house, and I saw what I thought were zeppelins in the sky. And I was absolutely frightened out of my wits.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they weren't zeppelins, they were barrage balloons. All over the sky I saw these what I thought were zeppelins and later found out that they weren't, they were barrage balloons, which I don't know if you know what they are. But they were to protect, I think, against low flying aircraft. When-- soon, when the blitz started-- the blitz of London, I mean-- I experienced part of that. But when it became really bad, my sister and I were evacuated to Ely in Cambridgeshire, where we were unfortunately separated. I was put in-- because nobody wanted two refugee kids or evacuees, I should say. So I was in one house, and she would be somewhere else. And we used to only see each other at school. I was there for, probably, I suppose a year and a half or something like that. But I became very ill there through neglect, and I was then sent to Cornwall, which is rather a long way from London. I was put into a Jewish children's home in Cornwall, and I really never saw my sister. I think I saw her once during the whole period until 1945, when the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay with your sister at-- in Hampstead Garden Suburb?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Hampstead Garden Suburb, I can't-- I don't exactly know. But it must be about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably a year or a year and a half, I suppose. I can't remember exactly when the blitz started, but that's got to be on record somewhere. And-- and I experienced part of the blitz. I do remember the air raids and we had an air raid shelter. We used to go in there every night, or nearly every night, and daytime often. And we all had our gas masks, which we used to have to carry around with us. We used to have to take them wherever we went. And as I say, when the blitz became very bad, we were then sent into the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about the background of these three sisters, why did they take--

speaker: interviewee
text: The three sisters were born in England-- there were four, actually. One was married. There were four sisters of Russian, I think, immigrants. The three that I lived with, my-- my guardian was unfortunately mental. She was actually in and out of mental homes, which I didn't know at the time. I only learned that when I-- when I came back to London later I realized it because I think I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Anne Morris. She-- the oldest sister was Sarah and the middle sister was Jane. And there was another sister called Ester, but she was married. Who-- I was particularly fond of her. She seemed to understand children more than-- than the other three, although they were as good as they could be to us. They just didn't quite know, I think, how to cope with us. And they-- Auntie Anne was very strict and punished-- punished us easily if we didn't do something that she thought was correct. I remember one particular incident, I couldn't have been more than nine years old. They had visitors in the house, and I was told to get ready for bed and come down in my dressing gown and say goodnight. And I was very embarrassed by this. I wanted to say goodnight before, but she wouldn't allow it. So I had to stand in the corner in the kitchen until I said goodnight. And I think I gave in after about an hour [LAUGHS] and came down in my dressing gown and said goodnight. And there were other little incidents that I remember where she-- I don't think she ever hit me, but she just punished us badly, like that, which was-- I-- I wasn't used to that. I was-- I was smacked, but I wasn't stood in-- I didn't stand in corners for ages. Another time, I was punished because she thought I had taken some sweets without asking. For three days she punished me until my sister took pity, because she had taken them, and admitted it. And little things, funny little incidents like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they seemed very old in my eyes, but they probably were ranging from 40 upwards. I'm guessing, I-- I don't really know. They looked very old to me. But I don't think they were as old as I imagined.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your sister feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was a very self-contained girl. She was very studious, and she spent most of her spare time hiding somewhere reading, away from me because-- so I didn't annoy her. So she-- she was a very-- very self-contained in that respect. I don't know how much she suffered, but she immersed herself in books and studying. She was a very studious girl. Not-- not me, I wanted to play all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- you-- you played a lot in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember somebody gave me a toy pedal car. And I remember once Auntie Anne, my guardian, had a business that did embroidery which was in the center of London. And I remember being taken there a few times and somebody gave me 15 shillings, which was an enormous amount of money. And I immediately persuaded her, or someone, to take me to Hamleys to spend this money and I immediately bought a doll because I loved dolls. I took them all to pieces to see how they were made. But I don't think I ever managed to get them together. But I also liked sewing very much. So I used to make a lot-- a lot of things. I made a lot of toys myself and did, as I said, a lot of sewing. I think that I wasn't a studious child. I spent most of my time sewing and playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the-- who were your friends at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my first little boyfriend. Apparently, he was not Jewish. He was the son of the man that built the houses where I lived. He was a builder. Apart from that, I-- I know I went to Hebrew classes and I had to go to synagogue. And the synagogue still exists today. I can't remember my-- even my schoolmates. I cannot remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in touch with other refugee children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so. We weren't. They-- they wanted-- she wanted us very much-- oh, I do remember one other child who was a lot younger than me and I was very envious of her. They were friends of the family, and I remember the parents of the child. And I remember the girl because I loved the name. She had a name-- her name was [? Carol, ?] and she was a lot younger than me. And she was a pretty little blonde girl. But apart from that, I really don't remember any other children. I must have obviously mixed with other children, I'm sure, because going to Hebrew classes and so on, I must have mixed with other children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other refugee children at your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think I was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other Jewish children at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. I remember-- no, I don't-- I don't think so. I don't know. I certainly can't remember. But I must have mixed with Jewish children, I'm sure, because of going to cheder. Every Sunday I had to go to cheder, which I didn't like very much. And I remember Pesach. And I even have today still a siddur, because we went I think to the rabbi's house once. We were invited for one Pesach, and I found the [HEBREW]. And for some unknown reason, which goes against my character, the prize-- the prize I asked for was a siddur, which I still have today, endorsed by the rabbi. So I must have mixed, obviously, with other Jewish children as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about the-- the language problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: The language problem. I think I picked English up very quickly. I remember some funny little incidents. I remember my sister going to Woolworth's and asking for a finger hat. Can you guess what that is? It's a thimble, literally translated from the German, [GERMAN]. And of course, they didn't know what she was talking about and that was a little joke for a long time afterwards. I think within six months, I spoke English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep on speaking German to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I don't think so. We weren't allowed. They-- Auntie Anne said we were only allowed to speak English, you've got to learn English. So we were not allowed to speak German. It came to the point in-- I remember in about 1942 when I was evacuated, I was in one particular house where the man of the house who was born in Germany-- he was not-- not a Jew-- but came to England when he was quite young. And he asked me simple questions, what is knife, what is spoon, in German, and I couldn't tell him. I literally forgot all my German until I mixed with German-speaking people after the war when I picked my-- when it came back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the sisters speak German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't speak German. They may have spoke-- I think they-- they spoke Russian. And in fact, I remember we had a dog. I hated it because I used to have to take this dog for a walk every day, and he used to take me for a walk. He was a very strong little dog and his name was [? Julic, ?] which is Russian, and it means rascal. I think they spoke some Russian. I don't think they even spoke Yiddish. I don't remember hearing any Yiddish until I married. My first mother-in-law was Polish, Polish origin, and I learned my Yiddish from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you hear from your parents when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very little. We received some Red Cross letters. I think they were allowed to write about 20 words and most of those were crossed out, heavily crossed out. So you could just about read-- read in between the lines what they-- what they had written. And-- and I remember they once sent a-- at the beginning, I-- I received a parcel. It must have been before the war. I can't remember what was in the parcel, but they probably were very short of money by then so they couldn't really send. The Red Cross letters, I must add, I think came after the war had started. We must have had letters before, but unfortunately, I haven't got any. The only letter I have from my mother was a letter I have been given, I was given about a year ago, which we came across by chance. My-- I have a much older cousin living in Canada, and we went through his papers and he allowed me to take this letter because one side-- it's from his mother, actually, who is my-- my mother's sister. And when we turned it over, we found a letter from my mother, which I have today. But I have no other correspondence, unfortunately, from them-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that-- in that time, you and your sister, neither, she didn't-- there was no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister, neither-- she didn't-- there was no correspondence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there must been. I haven't got any, though. My sister and I were together-- well, we were together but in separate houses in Ely until I think it was 1942. And then I think I only saw her once until I came back to London in 1945, when the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about the-- when you were evacuated to Ely?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't got very pleasant memories of my evacuation. The first house they put me in was a very nice house with quite affluent people, not Jews. I think they'd never seen Jews in Ely before. We were the first sort of Jews that came there. They-- I stole a biscuit so they threw me out because I wouldn't admit to it. But I-- they discovered that I had stolen this biscuit and I couldn't-- I was too frightened to admit to it so they got rid of me. And I was then put into a-- another private house with a very elderly couple, not Jewish of course. And there, through neglect I became very ill. And I was neglected not just by them, but by the doctor. And the only reason that saved me was our teacher that was in charge of our health who happened to be away at the time when I became so ill. And about a week later, she came back from her holiday, took one look at me, and rushed me to hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there they operated immediately. I had been so neglected that-- I don't know if this is relevant to the-- do you want to hear all this? You do. I had been so neglected that I had an ornery appendix which had already burst. And in those days, they had no drugs like we have today. There was penicillin, but it was only for the Army and the armed forces. I had peritonitis, I had double pneumonia, I had a kidney problem, my lungs collapsed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only-- the one that saved me was my mad Auntie Anne. She came from London, stayed in the hospital, and forced them to operate a second time, which actually saved my life. I was in hospital about-- I think about two months. And then, because I was so very frail and ill, I was sent to Cornwall, which is-- to Perranporth, Cornwall, which is by the sea. And I was put into the-- now I was in a Jewish children's home. It was a Jewish children's home evacuated from London, and there I was with Jewish children. For the rest of the war I stayed there. I missed about a year's schooling. We just went to a local village school in Perranporth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the first period of evacuation, you were still in touch with your Auntie--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean with the sisters with the-- with your-- with your guardian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes they must have obviously been in touch with me because she came-- she came to the hospital and looked after me in the hospital. She stayed in-- they allowed her to stay in the hospital to look after me. I remember that. And I have an older cousin who also came to see me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He lived in-- in Blackburn, in Lancashire. And-- and my-- I was in the hospital in Cambridge, and I remember my sister also, of course, came to see me. Cambridge is not far from Ely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other organizations you remember, representatives of organizations who came to see you or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. But I must say the-- the headmistress of the school was very kind. And you couldn't get things like oranges, bananas you couldn't get. And I remember they asked me what I wanted, and I asked for two things. I asked for a banana and a bicycle. The banana was not available, you couldn't get bananas. The bicycle I was given the money for. I had some rather wealthy very distant relatives living in Blackburn, and they sent me the 15 pound to buy a bicycle, which I never got [LAUGHS] because I wasn't allowed to do anything for more or less a year after that. Because don't forget, times have changed. You know, they kept you-- I couldn't walk when I got out of hospital because they kept you in bed. You couldn't-- you-- I could barely walk, I should say. I was lying in bed for about four weeks. I couldn't stand up for four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you related to-- you said you have distant family. Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my-- I called him Uncle Walter. The name [? Neumann, ?] Neuman. I believe he was a second cousin from on my-- of my mother's. And they came to England I think already in '33 and brought all their wealth with them. So they were-- they had-- they had their family with them, and I did visit them later on. They sent for me, and I visited them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't want to take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, which I think was very wrong, or they should have at least seen that we were all right. But they didn't. I don't think it was Uncle Walter's doing, but he had a wife that didn't want to know. She wasn't interested in-- in us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, was it ever discussed to go to this family, to live with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they sent money, the fare money, for me to visit them twice. And it was a very big eye-opener for me because they lived in a big house and they had servants. And I hadn't ever seen this, not since I was little anyway. It looked like a palace to me. But of course, it wasn't a palace, it was just a very nice, beautiful, ordinary home. But they didn't-- they weren't really- I think-- my uncle-- I called him Uncle, he wasn't, as I said explained, a real uncle. He was very kind, but she was not. His wife was not interested. She was only interested in her immediate own family. I also had some other distant relatives in London who also I think should have really-- god, maybe I shouldn't even say this. But it's not for general publication, so I can. They also came out with their money and were quite wealthy. They also didn't really help us when I think they should have done, I feel they could have done quite a bit. Because they did a lot of charity work, but not immediate charity work-- you know, not in-- not visible charity work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they? What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their-- their name was Worms. [GERMAN]. I'm sorry, I'm speaking German. She was also Lowenthal. Her maiden name was Lowenthal. And I think they were distantly related to my mother because, as I mentioned before, her name was Lowenthal as well. And they were-- they're very wealthy people living in London. The old lady is, of course, dead by now but the Lowenthals still exist here in London today.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that when you went to Ely that you were-- as a consequence of neglect, you ended up in the hospital. Why were you neglected? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody-- there was nobody to look after us. The elderly couple that I lived with, they weren't interested in-- in me as a-- as a human being. I think they just took their seven and six or whatever it was they got for us, I don't know. I think they were paid to take evacuees, but that's how far it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Just one. They just had one, me. There were no other children in that house. And it-- it's-- I-- I can't blame them. Because I remember, although I was very young-- I couldn't have been more than-- I must have been 11 and a half or so, I took myself to the doctor and I was in-- I had every symptom and I was in great pain. But he told me I-- I was imagining it and sent me off. And the next thing was that luckily, this teacher came back from her leave or holiday, whatever it was, looked at me, and just took me straight to hospital. She could see I was very ill. And nobody else seemed to care.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your sister exactly at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in Ely. And she told me afterwards she could see I was very ill, but she had-- but she had-- couldn't do anything. She was-- what could she do? She was only a child herself. She-- she told me I was-- I used to walk around-- I used to walk around doubled up in half, in pain and the usual things one has with appendicitis, the usual symptoms. Perhaps she told the teacher. Maybe she was the one that informed the teacher who came to see me and took me straight to hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her experience in the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, she stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know I don't know? I don't honestly know. Because we only seemed to meet at break times in school. I didn't-- don't-- I can't remember seeing much of her apart from that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's just about what I can remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the next step was that I was sent to Cornwall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been about '42, '43. And as I say, I spent the rest of my life-- of my-- of the war years, I should say, in Cornwall until V-E Day. When V-E Day was declared, the-- the children's home dispersed, and we were all sent back to London. Those that had relatives or a mother went to them, the ones that didn't were like me. I was put in another hostel, a B'nai B'rith hostel, in London, in Cricklewood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back. So you-- when you arrived in this- in the Jewish-- you said it was a Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I haven't got particularly happy memories [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of-- of the home because the-- the matron was very English, Jewish. I was on the whole well-treated because I was so puny and undernourished because I was-- I had been so ill. She really took quite a lot of care of me. And I remember not going to school for a whole year in the afternoons, I only went in the mornings and I had to rest every afternoon. These days that wouldn't happen, of course. But in those days, they still did that. I went to the local village school. The home, we were known as the Sun Ray girls and we-- we were always rather embarrassed because we had to wear red woolen pixie hoods, and we only were allowed to go out in groups. We didn't mix very much with local people. We weren't allowed. And Saturdays and Sundays, we were locked out in the afternoons and told to go for walks so that matron could rest. I don't know-- do you know I know so little about the war because we weren't allowed to listen to radios. She had radio, but we weren't allowed to listen to it. It was in her room. So I know very little about the war except what I've learned since, of course. Perhaps it was just as well because it wasn't such happy news, I think. Newspapers, I don't think we ever read them. We didn't have facilities. And of course, we had very little money. We went-- I think I used to get some pocket money. I think I used to get a shilling a week, of which I had to save sixpence and with of the sixpence I was-- I could buy perhaps some of my sweet ration or have my hair cut or go to the cinema, which they gave us a special rate. I think we used to spend three pennies-- old pennies. I'm talking about old money. And that was it. We didn't have any-- we could-- the only outings we had were if we could walk because there was no money available. We were short of clothing, it was all hand-me-downs, and very short of most things. But we were fed.
